do.narrate: The bedroom: where you spend eight hours a night and zero waking decisions. The blackout curtains are excellent at their job, which is why the succulent died. Somewhere in here, allegedly, are clothes suitable for the outside world.

onEnter.text: The bedroom. The bed notes your continued verticality with disapproval.

do.narrate: The bedroom has surrendered everything it was hiding — including the pants, which is the part history will remember. Nothing left in here but sleep debt. The front door is the next open ticket.

look.text: The way back to the living room and its field of dead appliances.

The closet's top shelf, bearing a cardboard ziggurat: cables, adapters, and — crowning it — four CRT monitors kept 'as backups.' The shelf brackets were rated for towels. The whole arrangement is a load-bearing question mark.

do.narrate: You reach for the bottom box. The ziggurat shifts — a slow, geological groan, forty pounds of vintage glass rethinking its position. You withdraw the hand. The shelf settles, unsatisfied.
You reach for the bottom box. The ziggurat shifts — a slow, geological groan, forty pounds of vintage glass rethinking its position. You withdraw the hand. The shelf settles, unsatisfied.
You reach for the bottom box. The ziggurat shifts — a slow, geological groan, forty pounds of vintage glass rethinking its position. You withdraw the hand. The shelf settles, unsatisfied.

look.text: The closet, doors open in permanent surrender. Contents: eleven hoodies in a gradient from 'conference swag' to 'load-bearing,' two collared shirts sealed in dry-cleaning plastic like evidence, and zero pants. The pants are not here. The pants have never been here.

open.text: It's already open. This closet gave up on privacy around the same time you did.

take.text: You own the hoodie you're wearing and the hoodie in your bag. A third would be hubris.

use.text: You riffle the hangers left to right. Cotton, cotton, fleece, regret, cotton. Nothing with a waistband that means business.

look.text: Blackout curtains, hotel-grade, hung so video calls would never backlight you. They've held this room at 2 a.m. for six years straight. Behind them, reportedly, is a sun.

do.narrate: You haul the curtains apart. Daylight enters like a SWAT team. The room, seen clearly for the first time in years, looks exactly like a place where a man has been for six years. You are now legally in a 'before' picture.

open.text: The curtains stand open. The room is still processing the exposure.

use.text: You adjust the curtain overlap by a centimeter, a maintenance ritual from the era of 4 a.m. standups with Singapore.

look.text: Your degree, framed, hung in the one room no camera ever sees. Bachelor of Science in a field that has been renamed four times since. The Latin is holding up better than the curriculum.

take.text: The diploma stays on the wall. It's the only certification you hold that never expires or requires renewal fees, and you treasure it for that.

look.text: The nightstand: a charging graveyard. Five cables for four dead standards, a phone dock shaped like optimism, and a paperback with a bookmark at page 12 that has not advanced since the first lockdown.

open.text: The drawer holds earplugs, a dead fitness band, and the user manual for the bed. The bed has a user manual. You closed the drawer years ago for a reason.

take.text: You need none of these cables. You keep all of these cables. This is the way.

look.text: A plastic alarm clock blinking 12:00, as it has since the power flicker of 2019. All day, every clock you own has been wrong in the cloud. This one has been wrong locally, dependably, for years. Seniority matters.

use.text: You could set it. But it's been 12:00 in this room for five years, and today of all days, you're not sure it's wrong.

take.text: The clock stays on duty. Somebody in this house has to keep the wrong time running.

do.narrate: You kneel and peer into the under-bed dark: dust with weather patterns, one sock (right), and — pushed against the wall — a vacuum-sealed space bag, contents compressed to two dimensions. A label in your own handwriting: 'HARD PANTS — DO NOT NEED.' Past you filed them in cold storage. Present you begs to differ.

look.text: The space bag waits in the dust, pants flattened inside like pressed flowers from a more formal civilization. Retrieval requires only commitment.
Dust, the sock, and a deflated bag. The archive has been emptied. The sock keeps its post.

do.narrate: You kneel and peer into the under-bed dark: dust with weather patterns, one sock (right), and — pushed against the wall — a vacuum-sealed space bag, contents compressed to two dimensions. A label in your own handwriting: 'HARD PANTS — DO NOT NEED.' Past you filed them in cold storage. Present you begs to differ.

You drag the bag out and break the vacuum seal. It inhales — a long, grateful gasp, five years of held breath — and the pants reinflate into the third dimension. Restore from cold storage: successful. Integrity check: they still fit, pending one brave test. You are holding Real Pants.

open.text: The bag is spent. It served the archive faithfully and now it rests.

use.text: The bag is spent. It served the archive faithfully and now it rests.

do.narrate: You kneel and peer into the under-bed dark: dust with weather patterns, one sock (right), and — pushed against the wall — a vacuum-sealed space bag, contents compressed to two dimensions. A label in your own handwriting: 'HARD PANTS — DO NOT NEED.' Past you filed them in cold storage. Present you begs to differ.

You drag the bag out and break the vacuum seal. It inhales — a long, grateful gasp, five years of held breath — and the pants reinflate into the third dimension. Restore from cold storage: successful. Integrity check: they still fit, pending one brave test. You are holding Real Pants.

take.text: You already performed the retrieval. The sock, per policy, is never coming out.

look.text: The bed, made to the standard of a man who knows nobody's coming to inspect it. The duvet is technical fabric. The pillow arrangement is load-balanced. Below deck, the under-bed dark keeps its own inventory.

use.text: Lie down? Now? The world's infrastructure is on fire and you're the only volunteer department in range. The bed will keep. The bed always keeps.

open.text: You lift the duvet, confirm the bed contains bed, and re-secure the perimeter.

take.text: You cannot take the bed with you, a sentence you may repeat to yourself at hour nine of this.

look.text: The dresser. Top drawer: socks, sorted by attrition. Middle drawer: t-shirts from conferences that no longer exist, for products that no longer exist, from companies that were acquired for the t-shirt budget. Bottom drawer: the soft-pants archive.

open.text: You pull the bottom drawer: joggers, sweatpants, and one pair of 'dress joggers,' a garment whose name should have been a warning. Soft pants, every one. Soft pants cannot make this journey. The town would smell the elastic.

take.text: You decline to pack joggers as a backup. If the Real Pants fail, you fail with them. Commitment is a deployment strategy.

use.text: You square up the sock drawer by reflex. Order must live somewhere today.

look.text: The Chair: neither closet nor hamper but a third state of matter, holding clothes too clean to wash and too worn to hang. Archaeologists would call it a midden. You call it the staging environment.

take.text: You will not find Real Pants in the staging environment. Nothing in staging is ever production-ready. You know this professionally.

use.text: You consider processing the pile. The pile considers being processed. You reach a mutual understanding: later.
